result:
The Peculiar Art of Getting Lost

There's something to be said for getting lost—not metaphorically, though that has its merits too, but genuinely, physically lost. In an age where our phones perpetually whisper our exact coordinates to satellites orbiting miles above, where we can summon detailed directions to virtually anywhere with a few taps, the experience of true disorientation has become as rare as handwritten letters or unprogrammed silence.

I discovered this recently when my phone died during a solo hike in unfamiliar woods. The trail, which had seemed so clear and well-marked just moments before, suddenly branched into a dozen uncertain possibilities. Each path looked equally traveled, equally plausible. The trees, which I'd barely noticed while following the blue blazes on my screen, suddenly became individuals—that gnarled oak, this cluster of birches, each potentially a landmark I should have been memorizing.

The first emotion wasn't fear but something more like awakening. How long had it been since I'd needed to truly observe my surroundings? Not just photograph them for Instagram or note them in passing, but really see them with the kind of attention our ancestors must have used constantly—the kind that meant survival.

Getting lost strips away a particular modern arrogance. We move through the world wrapped in an invisible bubble of certainty, always knowing where we are, where we're going, how long it will take to get there. We've traded our internal compasses for external ones, and in doing so, we've atrophied muscles we didn't know we had. When that bubble pops, when the crutch is kicked away, we're left with just ourselves and the world, unmediated.

I spent two hours "lost" that day, though I was never in real danger—the park wasn't large enough for that. But those two hours taught me more about the square mile of forest I wandered through than the previous ten visits combined. Without a destination, every direction became equally interesting. Without a schedule, I could stop to watch a pileated woodpecker methodically dismantling a dead tree, its prehistoric call echoing through the canopy.

Getting lost forces presence. You can't autopilot when you don't know where you're going. Every sense sharpens: the sound of water that might indicate a stream to follow, the angle of sunlight filtering through leaves, the subtle changes in vegetation that might signal a shift in elevation or proximity to civilization. Your body remembers skills it never quite forgot—reading the landscape, building mental maps, noting the way you came so you can reverse it if needed.

There's a Czech word, "litost," which Milan Kundera described as a state of agony and torment created by the sudden sight of one's own misery. I think there should be a word for the opposite—the sudden joy of discovering you're more capable than you thought. That's what getting lost gave me: the realization that beneath my technological dependencies, the old hardware still functioned. I could still navigate by sun and slope, still find my way by patient observation rather than satellite guidance.

This extends beyond the woods. We're lost-averse in every aspect of our lives. We curate our media consumption through algorithms that know what we want before we do. We choose restaurants based on reviews from strangers, removing the possibility of serendipitous discovery. We optimize our commutes, our workouts, our meal prep, our sleep schedules, all in service of never wasting a moment, never taking a wrong turn.

But wrong turns are where the interesting things happen. The restaurant with no reviews might serve the best soup you've ever tasted. The unfamiliar neighborhood you wander into while killing time might contain a bookstore that changes your life. The conversation you fall into with a stranger because you asked for directions might lead to a friendship, a job opportunity, or simply a moment of genuine human connection in an increasingly disconnected world.

I'm not advocating for recklessness. There are times and places where getting lost can be genuinely dangerous, where preparation and navigation tools are essential for safety. But I am suggesting that we've perhaps overcorrected, that in our quest to never be lost, we've forgotten the value of occasionally not knowing where we are.

Children understand this intuitively. Watch a child explore a new playground or a patch of woods. They don't move efficiently from point A to point B. They meander, investigate, double back, create elaborate imaginary geographies. They get lost in the best possible way, absorbed so completely in the act of discovery that location becomes irrelevant.

We could learn from them. We could occasionally leave our phones behind, or at least resist the urge to immediately GPS our way out of uncertainty. We could choose the less efficient route, the one that winds through unfamiliar streets. We could enter stores we've never noticed before, strike up conversations with people we'll never see again, say yes to invitations that don't quite fit our plans.

Getting lost is a small act of rebellion against the tyranny of optimization. It's a vote for mystery in an increasingly mapped world. It's practice for bigger uncertainties—because life, despite our best efforts, remains fundamentally unpredictable. The GPS can tell you where you are, but it can't tell you who you are or where you're really going in any sense that matters.

That day in the woods, I eventually found my way back to a familiar trail. My relief was tinged with something like disappointment. The spell was broken; I was no longer lost, just another hiker heading back to the parking lot. But I carried something with me—a reminder that the world is still bigger than our tools for managing it, that there's value in not always knowing exactly where we stand, that sometimes the best discoveries come from wandering off the marked path.

Now, when I hike, I sometimes deliberately take unfamiliar turns. Not dangerously so, but enough to feel that small thrill of uncertainty. I'm practicing being lost, building those navigation muscles back up. Because in a world that's increasingly mapped, measured, and predicted, the ability to embrace disorientation might just be a survival skill worth cultivating.
